name: Adi Sankara
dates_notes: Traditional dates from sectarian chronicles place him 788–820; academic consensus (Hacker, Mayeda) is somewhat earlier. The securely-authored corpus is much smaller than the traditional ascription set.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhāskara explicitly attacks Śaṅkara's māyā as a smuggled-in Buddhist concept; the polemic runs both directions in the commentarial tradition.
Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya is the principal vehicle for the refutation of Bhartṛprapañca's bhedābheda.

core_thesis: Śaṅkara argues, in *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1 and across the four *adhyāyas* of that commentary, that *brahman* alone is real. *Brahman* is *sat-cit-ānanda*, partless, and in its highest description *nirguṇa* (without determinate attributes). The *jīva* is non-different from *brahman* in essence — the position is fixed by the *mahāvākya tat tvam asi*, on which Śaṅkara's *Chāndogya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* 6 is the *locus classicus*. The phenomenal world is *mithyā*: not *asat* (sheer non-existent), and not *sat* in the way *brahman* is, but *anirvacanīya* — indeterminable on the binary — and sublatable in *brahma-jñāna*. The mechanism of apparent multiplicity is *adhyāsa* (superimposition). The *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* prefacing BSB 1.1.1 analyzes this: the *anātman* is mistakenly superimposed on the *ātman* and conversely, generating the entire transactional field of *pramātṛ*-*pramāṇa*-*prameya*. Cosmologically Śaṅkara defends *vivarta-vāda* (apparent transformation) against the *pariṇāma-vāda* of Sāṅkhya and Bhāskara — most fully at BSB 2.1. Liberating cognition is *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti*, arising from *mahāvākya*-instruction. *Karma* is preparatory and cannot of itself produce *mokṣa*, because *karma* presupposes the agent–object division that *jñāna* dissolves. The *jīvanmukta* lives out the residue of *prārabdha-karma* in a body whose phenomenality has ceased to be cognitively binding.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sutra-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the school-defining commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus on Śaṅkara's authorship.

engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's school-defining *bhāṣya* on Bādarāyaṇa's *Brahma-Sūtra*, organized by the four *adhyāyas* and their *pādas*. The work opens with the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* — Śaṅkara's prefatory analysis of *adhyāsa* (superimposition) as the universal pre-philosophical condition that makes ordinary cognition, and Vedic injunction itself, possible. The doctrinal hinges fall in successive loci. BSB 1.1.4 (*tat tu samanvayāt*) defends the thesis that *brahman* is the *tātparya* (settled purport) of all Upaniṣadic statements, against the Mīmāṃsaka thesis of *karma-pradhānya*. BSB 2.1 refutes the Sāṅkhya *pradhāna* and the *pariṇāma-vāda* alternative, and defends *vivarta*. BSB 2.2 refutes, in turn, Vaiśeṣika atomism, the Buddhist schools (Vijñānavāda, Sautrāntika, Vaibhāṣika, Mādhyamika), Jaina *anekānta*, Pāśupata, and Pāñcarātra. BSB 3.2 analyzes the *avasthās* and the *jīva*'s identity with *brahman*. BSB 4 treats *kramamukti*, *jīvanmukti*, and the trajectory of the *mukta-puruṣa*. Securely authored; the cornerstone of every later Advaita sub-school.

engaged_works.title: Bhagavad-Gita-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Śaṅkara's mature period; relative chronology with the Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya disputed but the two are clearly companion works.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the school-defining Gītā commentary and one of the four uncontested *bhāṣyas* of Śaṅkara.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus; Hacker, Mayeda, and Halbfass all treat it as authentic.

engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's prose *bhāṣya* on the *Bhagavad-Gītā* — the principal Vedānta Gītā-commentary and the textual anchor that establishes the *Gītā* as a *prasthāna* on equal footing with *Upaniṣad* and *Brahma-Sūtra*. The work opens with a celebrated *upodghāta* arguing that the *Gītā*'s purport (*tātparya*) is *jñāna*-mediated *mokṣa* and that *karma* and *jñāna* belong to mutually exclusive *adhikāra*-orders — *karma* being preparatory, never co-instrumental in the rise of liberating cognition. Major doctrinal loci include the analysis of *sāṃkhya*/*yoga* in chapter 2, the resolution of the apparent *karma*-injunction at 3.8 (*niyataṃ kuru karma*) as addressed to the still-unprepared seeker, the reading of *kṣetra-kṣetrajña* (chapter 13) as the *adhyāsa*-structure of cognition, and the chapter-15 analysis of the *puruṣottama* as identical to *nirguṇa Brahman* under devotional aspect. The *bhāṣya* fixes the *jñāna-niṣṭhā* / *karma-niṣṭhā* polarity that every later Vedāntin — Bhāskara, Rāmānuja, Madhva — must contest or accept.

engaged_works.title: Chandogya-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Companion to the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya in the mature Upaniṣad-bhāṣya cycle.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; one of the ten principal Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas universally accepted as Śaṅkara's.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Chāndogya-Upaniṣad — the longest of the Sāmaveda Upaniṣads and the second-largest principal Upaniṣad after the Bṛhadāraṇyaka. Organized by *prapāṭhaka* and *khaṇḍa*, the *bhāṣya* treats the great pedagogical sequences of the text: the *Sad-Vidyā* of *Uddālaka* and *Śvetaketu* (chapter 6), with the celebrated *mahāvākya* *tat tvam asi* repeated nine times as the climactic instruction; the *Bhūma-Vidyā* of *Sanatkumāra* and *Nārada* (chapter 7); the *Dahara-Vidyā* of the heart-space (chapter 8); and the *Sāmavidyās* of the early chapters. Śaṅkara's analysis of *tat tvam asi* — particularly the *jahad-ajahal-lakṣaṇā* by which the apparently contradictory predication of *brahman* on *jīva* is resolved by partial-retention of meaning — is the *locus classicus* for the Advaitic doctrine of identity-instruction. The *bhāṣya* also stages a sustained refutation of *bhedābheda* readings of the *Sad-Vidyā* and rejects any *upādhi*-mediated reading that would soften the *aikya*-thesis. Sureśvara composed no separate *vārttika*; the *Bhāṣya* itself is the school's definitive treatment.

engaged_works.title: Aitareya-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Short commentary on the Aitareya-Upaniṣad of the Ṛgveda, one of the briefer principal Upaniṣads. The text is in three *adhyāyas*: the cosmogonic opening (*ātman*'s creation of the *lokas* and the *loka-pālas*); the analysis of the threefold birth of the *jīva* (gestational, post-natal, and the eschatological re-birth into the next world); and the climactic *prajñānam brahma* identification — one of the four canonical *mahāvākyas*. Śaṅkara reads the cosmogony as figurative (*arthavāda*) attached to the doctrinal core, treats the threefold-birth analysis as preparatory dis-identification of the *jīva* from gross corporeal embodiment, and gives the *prajñānam brahma* equation his standard treatment: *prajñāna* (consciousness) is *brahman* not as an attribute (*viśeṣaṇa*) but as essential nature (*svarūpa-lakṣaṇa*), continuous with the *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* of Taittirīya. The *bhāṣya* is brief but doctrinally pivotal — it establishes the consciousness-identification reading of the *mahāvākya* against any *prajñā*-as-*guṇa* alternative.

engaged_works.title: Prashna-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle; companion to the Muṇḍaka-Bhāṣya in the Atharvavedic group.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Praśna-Upaniṣad, the Atharvavedic Upaniṣad organized as six *praśnas* (questions) put to the sage Pippalāda by six disciples. Each *praśna* takes up a major topic of upaniṣadic *vidyā*: the origin of *prajā*; the relative ranking of *prāṇa* and the senses; the genesis and operation of *prāṇa* itself; the four states of consciousness (*jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, and the implicit fourth); the meditative power of the *praṇava* (*oṃkāra*); and the doctrine of the *ṣoḍaśa-kala-puruṣa* (the sixteen-fold *puruṣa* whose *kalās* dissolve in *brahman*-knowledge). Śaṅkara's *bhāṣya* treats the text as a graded pedagogical sequence ascending from cosmology to *praṇava*-meditation to terminal *puruṣa-vidyā*; the analysis of the four states in the fourth *praśna* prefigures the longer treatment in the Māṇḍūkya-Bhāṣya, and the *ṣoḍaśa-kala* analysis is the *locus classicus* for the dissolution of *upādhis* in *brahman*-cognition.

engaged_works.title: Mundaka-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Muṇḍaka-Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda, in three *muṇḍakas* of two *khaṇḍas* each. The Upaniṣad opens with the canonical *parā-vidyā* / *aparā-vidyā* distinction (1.1.4–5) — the higher knowledge by which the imperishable is known, the lower comprising the four Vedas with their auxiliaries — which Śaṅkara takes as decisive for the *jñāna* / *karma* hierarchy. The second *muṇḍaka* contains the celebrated *dvā suparṇā* verse (3.1.1, also at Ṛg-veda 1.164.20) of the two birds on one tree, which Śaṅkara reads as the *jīva*-*Īśvara* relation under *adhyāsa*: the eating bird is the apparently-distinct *jīva*, the watching bird is *brahman* as *sākṣin*, and *mokṣa* is the recognition that the eating bird is in truth the watching bird. The *bhāṣya* also fixes the Advaita reading of the *paramaṃ sāmyam* (Muṇḍaka 3.1.3) as identity rather than mere likeness. The text's compact verses make it pedagogically central in the Advaita curriculum.

engaged_works.title: Katha-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Kaṭha-Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda — the dialogue of *Naciketas* and *Yama* in two *adhyāyas* of three *vallīs* each. Śaṅkara reads the text as an integrated graded instruction: Yama's three boons stage the seeker's progressive renunciation of householder, heavenly, and finally self-knowledge goods; the central *vallīs* unfold the *ātma-vidyā* through the celebrated *rathakalpana* (chariot-allegory: *ātman* as rider, *buddhi* as charioteer, *manas* as reins, *indriyas* as horses, *viṣayas* as paths) and the analysis of the *aṅguṣṭha-mātra puruṣa*. The *bhāṣya* fixes the Advaita reading of *nāyam ātmā pravacanena labhyaḥ* (1.2.23) as the Upaniṣadic warrant for the doctrine that *ātma-jñāna* requires self-disclosure (*svayaṃ vivṛṇute tanūṃ svām*), not merely instructional or inferential effort — a *locus* for the school's later analyses of the role of grace and the spontaneity of *aparokṣa-jñāna*. The treatment of the *ātman*'s subtle quantification ('thumb-sized') as figurative rather than ontological is doctrinally pivotal.

engaged_works.title: Mandukya-Upanisad-Bhashya with Gaudapada-Karika-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed as a unified commentary on the twelve mantras of the Upaniṣad together with all four prakaraṇas of Gauḍapāda's Kārikā.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the unified commentary on the Upaniṣad and Gauḍapāda's Kārikā is the school's principal source on the four states of consciousness and on *ajāti-vāda*.
engaged_works.summary: Unified commentary on the twelve mantras of the Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad together with the four *prakaraṇas* of Gauḍapāda's *Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā* (*Āgama*, *Vaitathya*, *Advaita*, *Alātaśānti*). Śaṅkara treats the Upaniṣad's analysis of the four *pādas* of *oṃ* — corresponding to *jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, and *turīya* — as the canonical structural map of consciousness, with *turīya* as the non-relational *ātman* whose recognition is *mokṣa*. On the Kārikā he gives his most extended exposition of *ajāti-vāda* (the doctrine that nothing has ever truly come into being), of the *vaitathya* (unreality) of waking and dream as parallel *vyāvahārika* orders, and of the *alāta-cakra* (firebrand-circle) analogy by which apparent multiplicity is shown to be the trace of an unmoving consciousness. The text is the principal Advaita document on the four states and on the limit-doctrine of *ajāti*; modern scholarship (Hacker, King) has questioned the unity of the four *prakaraṇas* but Śaṅkara reads them as a single integrated treatise. Doctrinally pivotal for the analysis of *mithyā* as neither *sat* nor *asat* but as cognitively sublatable.

engaged_works.title: Kena-Upanisad-Bhashya (pada-bhashya and vakya-bhashya)
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Two distinct bhāṣyas on the same Upaniṣad; both transmitted under Śaṅkara's name.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Both *pada-bhāṣya* (word-by-word) and *vākya-bhāṣya* (sentence-by-sentence) are securely ascribed to Śaṅkara; the existence of two parallel commentaries on a single short Upaniṣad is itself unusual and characteristic of the text's importance in the school.
engaged_works.summary: Two distinct *bhāṣyas* on the Kena-Upaniṣad of the Sāmaveda — the *pada-bhāṣya* (a word-by-word gloss) and the *vākya-bhāṣya* (a sentence-by-sentence and topically-organized exposition). The Upaniṣad opens with the celebrated questions *keneṣitaṃ patati preṣitaṃ manaḥ* — by what is the mind impelled to its target? — driving toward the recognition that the mover behind every cognitive faculty is *brahman* itself, knowable not as an object but only as the *pratyag-ātman* of every act of cognition. Śaṅkara's signature reading is *yan manasā na manute yenāhur mano matam* (1.5): *brahman* is *avijñātaṃ vijānatām*, unknown to those who think they know it, and *vijñātam avijānatām*, known to those who know themselves not to know it — the apophatic *locus classicus* for the school. The second half of the Upaniṣad's *Yakṣa* allegory (the failure of Agni, Vāyu, and Indra to comprehend the *Yakṣa*-Brahman) is read as the figurative dramatization of the same point: *brahman* is the ground of agency that no agent can objectify.

engaged_works.title: Isha-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Īśa-Upaniṣad — the shortest of the principal Upaniṣads, in eighteen mantras, and uniquely embedded within the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā of the Śukla-Yajurveda (whence its alternate name *Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā-Upaniṣad*). Śaṅkara's *bhāṣya* gives the school's authoritative reading of the text's apparent prescription of *karma* alongside *vidyā* (mantras 9–11: *vidyāṃ ca avidyāṃ ca yas tad vedobhayaṃ saha*): he refuses the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* construal advocated by Bhartṛprapañca and others, reading the *avidyā* / *vidyā* pair instead as *karma* and *upāsanā* — both preparatory disciplines belonging to the householder *adhikārin*, neither itself constituting *brahma-vidyā*. The work is thus the single most important short text for the school's *jñāna-karma-vibhāga* doctrine, and Śaṅkara's specific reading of *vidyā* in the contested mantras becomes the standard reference in every later debate on the question. Mantras 4–8 give the celebrated paradoxical descriptions of the *ātman* (*tad ejati tad naijati*), which Śaṅkara reads as *adhyāsa*-talk rather than as positive ontology.

engaged_works.title: Upadesha-Sahasri
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; Mayeda's critical edition establishes a stable text.
engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's only securely authored independent (non-commentarial) *prakaraṇa*, in two parts: a *gadya* (prose) section in three chapters, often treated as the doctrinal core, and a *padya* (metrical) section in nineteen chapters that develops particular topics in verse. The work is pedagogically structured: it presents the teacher–pupil dialogue as the *sāmpradāyika* (lineage-transmitted) means of liberating instruction. Central topics: the analysis of *aham*-cognition, the *dṛk*–*dṛśya* discrimination (consciousness as *sākṣin* vs. its objects), the *adhyāsa* of *anātman* on *ātman*, the dismantling of the *kartṛtva*–*bhoktṛtva* attribution to the *ātman*, and the role of *mahāvākya*-instruction. Mayeda's 1973 critical edition (Tokyo) is the standard text; Mayeda's translation and analytic apparatus are the basis of modern academic engagement with what Śaṅkara himself counts as direct doctrinal exposition (rather than *bhāṣya*-occasioned commentary).

engaged_works.title: Brhadaranyaka-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's longest Upaniṣad-commentary, on the Bṛhadāraṇyaka — itself the largest of the principal Upaniṣads. Organized by *adhyāya* and *brāhmaṇa*; treats the great Yājñavalkya dialogues (with Maitreyī, Janaka, Gārgī, Uddālaka), the *neti neti* characterization of *ātman*, the *madhu-vidyā*, the *pañcāgni-vidyā* and the post-mortem trajectory of the *jīva*. The commentary is the principal vehicle for the refutation of Bhartṛprapañca's *bhedābheda* and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: Śaṅkara repeatedly stages and rebuts the *bhedābheda* reading of Yājñavalkya's enumerations, establishing the strict reading of *neti neti* as terminal apophasis, not as one wing of a dual-aspect doctrine. Śaṅkara's analysis of the three *avasthās* in the dialogue with Janaka (Bṛh. 4.3) is the *locus classicus* for the Advaita treatment of dream and deep-sleep epistemology. Sureśvara's massive *Vārttika* takes this *bhāṣya* as its base. Securely authored.

engaged_works.title: Taittiriya-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Taittirīya-Upaniṣad in three *vallīs*: *Śīkṣā-Vallī* (phonetics, *vidyās*, the introductory *anuvākas*), *Brahmānanda-Vallī* (*Ānanda-Mīmāṃsā*: the *pañca-kośa* analysis of *ātman* through five sheaths — *anna-maya*, *prāṇa-maya*, *mano-maya*, *vijñāna-maya*, *ānanda-maya* — and the famous *brahman*-defining text *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma*), and *Bhṛgu-Vallī* (the dialogue between Varuṇa and Bhṛgu yielding the same *kośa*-progression as a meditative ascent). Śaṅkara's analysis of the *kośa* hierarchy is doctrinally pivotal: he treats *ānanda-maya-kośa* as still belonging to the order of upādhi, not as *brahman* itself — a reading explicitly opposed to Rāmānuja's later identification of *ānanda-maya* with the supreme. The exegesis of *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* (Tait. 2.1.1) is the standard Advaita *locus* for the doctrine that *brahman*'s positive characterizations (*sat*, *cit*, *ānanda*) are *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa*, not *guṇas* in the Vaiśeṣika sense. Sureśvara's *Taittirīya-Vārttika* is the standard sub-commentary.

engaged_works.title: Vivekachudamani
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita living tradition; modern scholarship divided, with several scholars (e.g., Grimes' defense vs. Hacker's skepticism) actively debating authorship.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *prakaraṇa* in approximately 580 verses (manuscript count varies), structured as the systematic instruction of a qualified seeker through the standard Advaita pedagogical sequence: *sādhana-catuṣṭaya* (the four prerequisites — *viveka*, *vairāgya*, the *ṣaṭ-sampatti*, *mumukṣutva*); *śravaṇa*–*manana*–*nididhyāsana*; the *pañca-kośa-viveka*; the *avasthā-traya-viveka*; the *dṛk*–*dṛśya-viveka*; the *mahāvākya* analysis culminating in *jīvanmukti*. The work is the single most influential Advaita primer in the living tradition. Academic ascription is divided: Hacker's stylometric work argues against Śaṅkara's authorship, while John Grimes (2004) defends it. The Advaita tradition embraces it as Śaṅkara's; the school-ascribed status here registers both that embrace and the academic doubt. School-ascribed; the Advaita tradition embraces this as Śaṅkara's; its devotional and meditatively-instructive register is part of how Advaita-as-living-tradition presents itself, distinct from the strictly *bhāṣya*-based academic Śaṅkara.

engaged_works.title: Aparokshanubhuti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita living tradition; modern academic ascription divided.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *prakaraṇa* in 144 verses on direct, immediate (*aparokṣa*) realization of *ātman*. Structurally compact: opens with the *sādhana-catuṣṭaya*, develops a sequence of *vicāra*-questions through which the seeker disidentifies the *ātman* from body, *prāṇa*, *manas*, *buddhi*, and *ahaṃkāra*, then sets out a fifteen-limbed (*pañcadaśa-aṅga*) *yoga* — explicitly described in *aṣṭāṅga*-style language but with each limb redefined non-dually (e.g., *yama* as restraint of organs in the recognition of *brahman*-identity, *āsana* as steadiness in *brahman*-cognition, *prāṇāyāma* as cessation of objectifying mental movement). The work's heavy use of *yogic* technical vocabulary in an Advaitic key is part of why academic scholarship has been hesitant to ascribe it to the *bhāṣya*-author, while the same feature is precisely what makes it valued by the Advaita tradition as a practical meditation manual. School-ascribed; the Advaita tradition embraces this as Śaṅkara's.

engaged_works.title: Saundarya-Lahari
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita and Śrīvidyā living traditions; academic opinion divided on authorship of part or all of the hymn.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *stotra* in 100 verses (in the standard recension) addressed to the Goddess as *Tripurasundarī* / *Lalitā*, the supreme Śakti of the Śrīvidyā tradition. Traditionally divided into two halves: *Ānanda-Laharī* (verses 1–41) on the metaphysical-theological exposition of the Goddess as identical with the supreme, including *śrīcakra* and *kuṇḍalinī* references; and *Saundarya-Laharī* proper (42–100) on the *aṅga-varṇana* — limb-by-limb iconographic celebration of the Goddess's form. The text is the central liturgical poem of the Śrīvidyā tradition and a major Sanskrit *stotra* in its own right. Academic ascription is contested: many scholars argue at least the second half is post-Śaṅkara; the tradition embraces the whole as Śaṅkara's. School-ascribed; the Advaita tradition embraces this as Śaṅkara's; its Viśiṣṭādvaita-flavored devotional language and its embrace of a personal-theistic Śakti as supreme is a load-bearing piece of evidence for the comparative-claims framework about whether Advaita-as-living-tradition is as gap-distant from Viśiṣṭādvaita as Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya alone suggests.

engaged_works.title: Bhaja-Govindam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita living tradition; uses Vaiṣṇava devotional idiom that the academic ascription set is wary of attributing to the bhāṣya-author.
engaged_works.summary: Also called *Mohamudgara* ('Hammer for Delusion'). A short *stotra* of approximately 31 verses (with sub-recensions ascribing the additional verses to Śaṅkara's twelve *śiṣyas*), framed as a teaching to a grammar-pedant overheard rote-drilling Pāṇinian rules in old age. The opening refrain — *bhaja govindaṃ bhaja govindaṃ govindaṃ bhaja mūḍha-mate / saṃprāpte sannihite kāle nahi nahi rakṣati ḍukṛñ-karaṇe* — explicitly subordinates technical learning to *Govinda*-*bhakti* in the face of mortality. The *vairāgya* register and the choice of *Govinda* (a Vaiṣṇava name) as the addressed deity are doctrinally pivotal: they show the Advaita tradition not merely tolerating but actively cultivating personal-theistic devotional address as a *sādhana*. School-ascribed; the Advaita tradition embraces this as Śaṅkara's; its Viśiṣṭādvaita-flavored devotional language is a load-bearing piece of evidence for the comparative-claims framework about whether Advaita-as-living-tradition is as gap-distant from Viśiṣṭādvaita as Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya alone suggests.

engaged_works.title: Sivananda-Lahari
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita and Śaiva living traditions; academic opinion divided.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *stotra* in 100 verses to Śiva, in highly ornate Sanskrit (heavy use of *śleṣa*/double-meaning, elaborate metaphor cycles — the bee-and-lotus, the river-and-ocean — for the *bhakta*'s longing for and absorption into Śiva). Doctrinally the work is unambiguously *bhakti*-saturated: the speaker presents himself as *dāsa* of Śiva, repeatedly affirms the personal-theistic relation, and treats *Śivānanda* — the bliss of the personal Lord — as the seeker's *paramārtha*-fruit. The hymn is paired by tradition with the Saundarya-Laharī (Goddess) and Bhaja-Govindam (Viṣṇu/Govinda) as the school's three principal devotional anchors across the three theistic streams. School-ascribed; the Advaita tradition embraces this as Śaṅkara's; its Viśiṣṭādvaita-flavored devotional language and its frank embrace of *bhakti* as the operative *sādhana*-register is a load-bearing piece of evidence for the comparative-claims framework about whether Advaita-as-living-tradition is as gap-distant from Viśiṣṭādvaita as Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya alone suggests.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
na tato 'mṛtatāśāsti /
karmaṇo 'jñānahetutaḥ /
mokṣasya jñānahetutvān /
na tadanyad apekṣate //

pada_analysis.gloss: because it has ignorance as its cause
because of having knowledge as its cause

karaka_structure.note: Subject of asti.
Ablative source-domain: 'from action'.
Ablative of reason explaining why karma cannot yield immortality.
Implicit subject understood from mokṣasya in the second hemistich.
Object not depended upon.
Ablative of reason: mokṣa has jñāna as its causal condition.

key_passages.english_close: From that there is no hope of immortality, because action has ignorance as its cause; because mokṣa has knowledge as its cause, it does not depend on anything else.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: action, especially prescribed ritual or duty)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not a stray maxim but a causal thesis in miniature: Śaṅkara explicitly ties karma to the ignorance-governed order and mokṣa to jñāna alone. It fairly represents his position because the denial of jñāna-karma-samuccaya is a standing commitment across his secure corpus, and here it is stated without dependence on later sub-school terminology.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext of Upadeśasāhasrī based on Sengaku Mayeda, Śaṅkara's Upadeśasāhasrī, vol. 1 (Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass, 2006; 1st ed. Tokyo, 1973), materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/shankara_upadesa_sahasri_gretil.txt.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
anyadṛṣṭis tv avidyā syāt /
tannāśo mokṣa ucyate /
jñānenaiva tu so 'pi syād /
virodhitvān na karmaṇā //

karmakāryas tv anityaḥ syād /
avidyākāmakāraṇaḥ /
pramāṇaṃ veda evātra /
jñānasyādhigame smṛtaḥ //

jñānaikārthaparatvāt taṃ /
vākyam ekaṃ tato viduḥ /
ekatvaṃ hy ātmano jñeyaṃ /
vākyārthapratipattitaḥ //

pada_analysis.gloss: having ignorance and desire as its causes
because of being directed to one purport, namely knowledge

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: avidyā ca kāmaś ca kāraṇe yasya
jñānam eva eka arthaḥ; tasmin paratvaṃ yasya
samasa_vigrahas.note: A compressed abstract compound: 'because it is oriented to the single purport of knowledge'.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of syāt.
Predicate-nominative in passive with ucyate: 'is called mokṣa'.
What is called mokṣa in the passive construction.
Instrument by which mokṣa comes about.
Excluded instrument: not by karma.
Ablative of reason: karma is opposed.
Subject of syāt in the second verse.
Subject of the passive predicate smṛtaḥ.
Genitive relation with adhigame.
Domain in which the Veda functions as pramāṇa.
Object of viduḥ.
Ablative source: from grasping the sentence-meaning.

verb_modality.note: Occurs three times in the passage.

key_passages.english_close: The seeing of something else would be avidyā; the destruction of that is called mokṣa, and that too would come only through jñāna, not through karma, because of the opposition [between them]. What is produced by karma would be impermanent, having avidyā and desire as its causes. Here the Veda alone is held to be the pramāṇa for the attainment of jñāna; because it is directed to a single purport, they therefore know that sentence to be one, for the oneness of the self is to be known from understanding the sentence-meaning.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: action, especially prescribed ritual or duty)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This passage is unusually useful because Śaṅkara connects four load-bearing claims in one place: difference-seeing is avidyā, mokṣa is its destruction, karma yields only the impermanent, and Vedic sentence-meaning is the pramāṇa for non-dual knowledge. That combination makes it a fair representation of his epistemology and not a cherry-picked slogan.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext of Upadeśasāhasrī based on Sengaku Mayeda, Śaṅkara's Upadeśasāhasrī, vol. 1 (Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass, 2006; 1st ed. Tokyo, 1973), materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/shankara_upadesa_sahasri_gretil.txt.

key_passages.locus_long: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad 7, as glossed in Śaṅkara's bhāṣya
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
nāntaḥprajñaṃ na bahiḥprajñaṃ nobhayataḥprajñaṃ na prajñānaghanam /
na prajñaṃ nāprajñam /
adṛṣṭam avyavahāryam agrāhyam alakṣaṇam acintyam avyapadeśyam /
ekātmapratyayasāraṃ prapañcopaśamaṃ śāntaṃ śivam advaitaṃ caturthaṃ manyante /
sa ātmā sa vijñeyaḥ //

pada_analysis.gloss: whose essence is the conviction of one self

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: eka ātmā iti pratyayaḥ sāraṃ yasya
samasa_vigrahas.note: Śaṅkara's bhāṣya explicitly glosses this around the unwavering cognition 'eko 'yam ātmā'.

karaka_structure.note: Object-complement of manyante: the described reality is considered 'the fourth'.
Implicit subject of manyante.
Subject of the final nominal sentence.

key_passages.english_close: Not inwardly cognizing, not outwardly cognizing, not cognizing both ways, not a mass of cognition; not cognizing, not non-cognizing; unseen, not available for transaction, not graspable, without defining mark, unthinkable, not nameable; whose essence is the conviction of one self, the cessation of phenomenal proliferation, peaceful, auspicious, non-dual, they consider [that] the fourth. That is the self; that is to be known.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This mantra is famous because Śaṅkara makes it the apex-definition of the whole Māṇḍūkya teaching, not because later anthologies happened to favor it. In the supplied corpus it is the clearest compact locus for his strategy of denying every state-bound predicate while preserving the self as non-dual consciousness rather than collapsing it into non-being.
key_passages.source_edition: Existing local text / GRETIL plaintext, Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad with Māṇḍūkyamūlamantrabhāṣya, Gauḍapāda-Kārikā, and Śaṅkara bhāṣya, materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandukya_upanisad_gaudapada_shankara_bhasya_existing.txt; header notes the mūla checked against V. P. Limaye and R. D. Vadekar (Poona, 1958) and the Kārikā text against A. V. Kathavate (Poona, 1890).

key_passages.locus_long: Gauḍapāda-Kārikā 2.32, as glossed in Śaṅkara's bhāṣya
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
na nirodho na cotpattir /
na baddho na ca sādhakaḥ /
na mumukṣur na vai mukta /
ity eṣā paramārthatā //
key_passages.english_close: There is no cessation and no origination; no one bound and no practitioner; no seeker of liberation, and indeed no liberated one: this is the highest truth.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is worth selecting because Śaṅkara's own gloss at the same locus unpacks each negation as paramārthika rather than merely rhetorical. It therefore fairly represents his ajāti reading of causation and his insistence that bondage, practice, and release belong to the empirical teaching context rather than to Brahman's ultimate status.
key_passages.source_edition: Existing local text / GRETIL plaintext, Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad with Māṇḍūkyamūlamantrabhāṣya, Gauḍapāda-Kārikā, and Śaṅkara bhāṣya, materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandukya_upanisad_gaudapada_shankara_bhasya_existing.txt; header notes the mūla checked against V. P. Limaye and R. D. Vadekar (Poona, 1958) and the Kārikā text against A. V. Kathavate (Poona, 1890).
